essential_question: Can you use trig to find the height of something you cannot climb up and measure — and then turn that same real triangle into all six ratios?
objective: Build a clinometer, measure a real angle of elevation and baseline distance, solve for the height of a real object using tangent, and compute all six trigonometric ratios (reduced) from the resulting real, measured right triangle.

content.heading: From the sim to a real height
bullets: Yesterday: dragged θ on a screen and watched all six ratios flip.
Today: build a real clinometer, sight a real object, and solve a real height problem end to end.
The test: your computed height and your six computed ratios both come from the SAME real triangle — no made-up numbers.

content.heading: Gather + plan
content.note: Open the project card. Gather the protractor, straw, tape, string, weight, tape measure, and calculator. Pick a tall target you cannot climb (flagpole, hoop, tree, roofline) and a clear line of sight to its top.

content.heading: Measure the real triangle
bullets: Tape the straw along the protractor's straight edge; hang the weighted string from the center hole. Level the straw — the string should cross 90°.
Measure the baseline: the straight-line ground distance from your spot to directly below the target. This is your adjacent side.
Measure your eye height at the straw.
Sight the top of the target through the straw; have a partner read where the string crosses the scale.

content.heading: From one real triangle to six real ratios

content.preview: Session 3: defend the reciprocal relationships, review MUS Lesson Practice 30A/30B, and take the final mastery checkpoint.

success_criteria.text: I can build and use a clinometer to measure a real angle of elevation
I can use tangent, a measured baseline, and eye height to solve a real height/distance problem
I can compute all six trigonometric ratios, reduced, from a real measured right triangle, and verify each reciprocal pair multiplies to 1

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W34 The Six Trig Ratios.md

questions.prompt: Record your clinometer sighting: baseline distance (adjacent), eye height, and the angle of elevation θ you read (with units). Show reading − 90° = θ.
questions.answer: Any real, consistent set of measurements, e.g. baseline = 12 m, eye height = 1.5 m, protractor reading = 58°, so θ = |58° − 90°| = 32°.
questions.explain: Rubric: baseline recorded with units (1 pt) + angle correctly found as |reading − 90°| (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Using your baseline and θ, compute the height ABOVE your eye: height-above-eye = baseline × tan θ. Then add your eye height to get the target's total height. Show both numbers.
questions.answer: E.g. with baseline = 12 m and θ = 32°: height-above-eye = 12 × tan(32°) ≈ 7.50 m; total height ≈ 7.50 + 1.5 = 9.00 m. (Numbers will vary — the method is what's graded.)
questions.explain: tan θ = opposite/adjacent, so opposite (height above eye) = adjacent (baseline) × tan θ; the eye height must be added back on since the clinometer measures from eye level, not the ground.

questions.prompt: Compute the hypotenuse (your line-of-sight distance to the top) with the Pythagorean theorem, using your baseline and height-above-eye from the last question.
questions.explain: The baseline and height-above-eye are the two legs of the real right triangle you measured; the Pythagorean theorem gives the third side, the hypotenuse.

questions.prompt: Using your baseline (adjacent), height-above-eye (opposite), and hypotenuse from your own measurements, compute all six trig ratios for θ — sin, cos, tan, csc, sec, cot — as decimals, reducing any fraction that reduces. Then verify sin θ × csc θ ≈ 1 (or show one of the other two reciprocal checks).
questions.answer: E.g. with adjacent ≈ 12, opposite ≈ 7.50, hypotenuse ≈ 14.15: sin θ ≈ 0.530, cos θ ≈ 0.848, tan θ ≈ 0.625, csc θ ≈ 1.887, sec θ ≈ 1.179, cot θ ≈ 1.600. Check: sin θ × csc θ ≈ 0.530 × 1.887 ≈ 1.00. (Numbers will vary with each student's own measurements.)
questions.explain: Every ratio comes directly from the same three real, measured/computed side lengths — this is the whole lesson applied to a triangle you built with your own hands, not a textbook diagram.

questions.prompt: Reflect: where do you think the biggest source of error crept into your height calculation — the baseline measurement, the angle reading, or the eye height — and how would you reduce that error if you did this again?
